source_metadata.title_raw: Michael C. Ghizzoni  - A-18-264 - December 13, 2018 - Santa Barbara

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

December 13, 2018

Michael C. Ghizzoni
Santa Barbara County Counsel
105 E. Anapamu Street, Suite 201
Santa Barbara, CA 93101

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-18-264

Dear Mr. Ghizzoni:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Santa Barbara County Auditor-Controller-Elect Betsy Schaffer, regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"), as well as Government Code Section 1090.¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and Section 1090, not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest, including Public Contract Code.

Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

In regard to our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Santa Barbara County District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a written response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

QUESTION

Under the Act and Section 1090, does Auditor-Controller-Elect Betsy Schaffer's former marriage to the president of Simpler Systems, Inc. ("Simpler Systems"), disqualify her from taking part in governmental decisions involving that company, including contracts, once she takes office?

___________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-18-264
Page No. 2

CONCLUSION

No, neither the Act nor Section 1090 limit Ms. Schaffer from taking part in governmental
decisions involving Simpler Systems based on her previous marriage to the president of that
company, because she has no financial interest in the company or her former spouse.

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

Betsy Schaffer is scheduled to take office as County of Santa Barbara's Auditor-Controller
on January 7, 2019. Simpler Systems is a software company that provides software programs and
consulting services to multiple County departments, including the Auditor-Controller's Department,
of which Ms. Schaffer is soon to be the elected Department Head. Ms. Schaffer was formerly
married to the president of Simpler Systems, but divorced in August of 2013. Due to her prior
involvement with Simpler Systems, Ms. Schaffer has previously stayed "walled off" from any
contracts involving the company.

Ms. Schaffer has stated that she does not currently have any financial interest in or from the
company, and does not expect any future financial interest in or from the company. She does not
receive any spousal support from her former husband and does not receive any child support, as the
couple did not have any children. Ms. Schaffer performed hourly bookkeeping services for Simpler
Systems prior to her appointment as Assistant Auditor-Controller on June 6, 2016. Her last income
from the company was in April 2016.

ANALYSIS

The Act

Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from making, participating in making,
or in any way attempting to use her official position to influence a governmental decision in which
she knows or has reason to know she has a financial interest.

An official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from
its effect on the public generally, on those interests identified in Section 87103. As pertinent to the
facts provided, potentially disqualifying interest include the following:

• Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth
$2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a).)

• Any source of income aggregating $500 or more in value provided or promised to, received
by, the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.
(Section 87103(c).)

• A public official's own personal finances, or those of a member of his or her immediate
family. (Section 87103).)

File No. A-18-264
Page No. 3

Given that Ms. Schaffer is divorced from the president of Simpler Systems, he is no longer her "immediate family." Therefore, any reasonably foreseeable, material financial effect a governmental decision might have on him does not disqualify Ms. Schaffer from participating in making those decisions. (See Morales Advice Letter, No. A-99-246(a) ("If an official has obtained a final judgment of dissolution of marriage or legal separation of the parties, income received by the official's spouse is no longer attributable to the official under the Act."); Shea Advice Letter, No. A-99-223.)

Further, it has been longer than 12 months since Ms. Schaffer received income from Simpler Systems for her work with the company. (See Morales Advice Letter, No. A-99-246(a); Section 87103(c).) Therefore, Ms. Schaffer does not have an interest in Simpler Systems as a "source of income" that might otherwise disqualify her from taking part in governmental decisions involving the company.

Because neither Simpler Systems, nor Ms. Schaffer's former husband, currently qualify as "financial interests" for Ms. Schaffer, she is not disqualified from taking part governmental decisions involving Simpler Systems based upon the facts provided.²

Section 1090

Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section 1090 is concerned with financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officers from exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their agencies. (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Specifically, Section 1090 provides, in part, that "[m]embers of the Legislature, state, county, district, judicial district, and city officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any contract made by them in their official capacity, or by any body or board of which they are members." Section 1090 applies to officials who participate in any way in the making of the contract, including involvement in matters such as preliminary discussions, negotiations, compromises, reasoning, planning, drawing of plans and specifications, and solicitation for bids. (Milthrae Assn. for Residential Survival v. City of Millbrae (1968) 262 Cal.App.2d 222, 237; see also Stigall, supra at p. 569.) Section 1090 is intended "not only to strike at actual impropriety, but also to strike at the appearance of impropriety." (City of Imperial Beach v. Bailey (1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 197.)

Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has a financial interest," and officials are deemed to have a financial interest in a contract if they might profit from it in any way. (People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) Although Section 1090 does not specifically define the term "financial interest," case law and Attorney General opinions state that prohibited financial interests may be indirect as well as direct, and may involve financial losses, or the possibility of losses, as well as the prospect of pecuniary gain. (Thomson, supra, at pp. 645, 651-652; see also People v. Vallerga (1977) 67 Cal.App.3d 847, 867, fn. 5; Terry v. Bender (1956) 143 Cal.App.2d 198, 207-208; People v. Darby (1952) 114 Cal.App.2d 412, 431-432; 85 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34, 36-38 (2002); 84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 158, 161-162 (2001).)

2 Again, this is assuming—based on the facts presented—that Ms. Schaffer has no other ties to Simpler Systems that might establish a "financial interest" involving the company.

File No. A-18-8164
Page No. 4

However, because Simpler Systems is no longer a source of Ms. Schaffer's income and she
has no connection to her former spouse's finances, Ms. Schaffer would have no financial interest in
a contract involving Simpler Systems. (See Lexin v. Superior Court (2010) 47 Cal.4th 1050, 1090-
91 (stating that the Act and Section 1090 are "in pari materia," i.e., "on the same subject," and
therefore should be construed together; see also id., at p. 1091, citing 65 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 41, 57
(1982) (interpretation of what constitutes a "financial interest" under Section 1090 and the Act
should be consistent).) Accordingly, Section 1090 does not prohibit her from participating in
making governmental decisions, including contracts, involving that company.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Kevin Cornwall
Counsel, Legal Division

KMC:jgl

Under the Act and Section 1090, does Auditor-Controller-Elect Betsy Schaffer's former 
marriage to the president of Simpler Systems, Inc. ("Simpler Systems"), disqualify her from taking 
part in governmental decisions involving that company, including contracts, once she takes office?
sections.conclusion:
No, neither the Act nor Section 1090 limit Ms. Schaffer from taking part in governmental 
decisions involving Simpler Systems based on her previous marriage to the president of that 
company, because she has no financial interest in the company or her former spouse.

or in any way attempting to use her official position to influence a governmental decision in which

87103(c).) Therefore, Ms. Schaffer does not have an interest in Simpler Systems as a "source of

the company. 
Because neither Simpler Systems, nor Ms. Schaffer's former husband, currently qualify as

financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from 
exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their

specifications, and solicitation for bids. (Millbrae Assn. for Residential Survival v. City of Millbrae

432; 85 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34, 36-38 (2002); 84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 158, 161-162 (2001).) 
2 Again, this is assuming-based on the facts presented-that Ms. Schaffer has no other ties to Simpler 
Systems that might establish a "financial interest" involving the company.

However; because Simpler Systems is no longer a source of Ms. Schaffer's income and she

decisions involving Simpler Systems based on her previous marriage to the president of that
